title: How the Toolbox Works
text: The toolbox is designed to help users move from a question to a result.

title: How the Toolbox Works
text:
Step 1 – Define the assessment goal
Start by identifying the main issue of interest:

salinity,
soil organic carbon,
structure and compaction,
nutrient status,
water behaviour,
biodiversity,
or a broader soil-health overview.

title: How the Toolbox Works
text:
Step 2 – Choose the relevant tools
Select one or more tools that fit the use case:

rapid field methods for quick screening,
lab-supported methods for deeper analysis,
digital tools for recording, visualising, and comparing results.

title: How the Toolbox Works
text:
Step 3 – Collect observations or samples
Depending on the method, this may involve:

taking direct in-field measurements,
collecting soil samples,
recording geolocated observations,
capturing images,
or preparing material for laboratory analysis.

title: How the Toolbox Works
section: Step 4 – Store and structure the information
text:
Step 4 – Store and structure the information
Use the digital toolbox components to:

record field observations,
upload or organise lab information,
connect data to location,
and keep outputs ready for comparison and reporting.

title: How the Toolbox Works
text:
Step 5 – Interpret the results
Interpretation can involve:

direct values from field or lab methods,
comparison across plots, fields, or locations,
visualisation through maps and dashboards,
or summarisation through user-facing outputs.

title: How the Toolbox Works
text:
Step 6 – Produce user-facing outputs
Outputs may include:

site-level observations,
tables and charts,
mapped context,
and summary reports such as soil-health cards.

title: Soil Spectroscopy
text: Soil spectroscopy is a rapid, non-destructive method that uses the interaction between light and soil to estimate several soil properties.

title: Soil Spectroscopy
text:
Main target variables

soil organic carbon / organic matter,
texture-related variables,
pH-related variation,
moisture-sensitive interpretation,
and broader physicochemical screening.

title: Soil Spectroscopy
text:
Descriptor groups supported

soil organic carbon,
nutrient and fertility support,
general soil condition,
and support for repeated monitoring.

title: Soil Spectroscopy
text:
Why users may choose it
Spectroscopy is attractive when users want a fast, multi-variable measurement approach that can be repeated over time.

title: Salinity / pH / EC Methods
text: This toolbox area includes rapid methods and sensors used to screen the chemical condition of soil, especially for salinity-related and acidity-related questions.

title: Salinity / pH / EC Methods
text:
Main target variables

pH,
electrical conductivity,
salinity / TDS,
and related chemical screening variables.

title: Salinity / pH / EC Methods
text:
Descriptor groups supported

salinity,
acidity,
nutrient-related screening,
and general soil-chemistry interpretation.

title: Macrofauna Observation
text: Macrofauna observation focuses on visible soil fauna, including earthworms and other larger organisms that can be collected, observed, counted, or imaged.

title: Macrofauna Observation
text:
Main target variables

earthworm abundance,
visible macrofauna groups,
image-based specimen records,
and broader biological field observations.

title: Macrofauna Observation
text:
Descriptor groups supported

biodiversity,
biological functioning,
and support for ecological soil-health interpretation.

title: Infiltration Test
text: Infiltration testing helps users assess how quickly water enters the soil. It is a practical field method for understanding water movement and related structural behaviour.

title: Infiltration Test
text:
Main target variables

infiltration rate,
water entry behaviour,
support for porosity and hydraulic interpretation.

title: Infiltration Test
text:
Descriptor groups supported

reduced water retention / water behaviour,
structure,
compaction support,
and practical field comparison.

title: eDNA / Metabarcoding
text: eDNA and metabarcoding workflows provide a deeper laboratory view of soil biological communities.

title: eDNA / Metabarcoding
text:
Main target variables

fungal diversity,
bacterial diversity,
archaeal diversity,
and selected functional-group indicators depending on the workflow.

title: eDNA / Metabarcoding
text:
Descriptor groups supported

biodiversity,
biological functioning,
and advanced ecological interpretation.

title: eDNA / Metabarcoding
text:
Practical note
This is a more specialised toolbox component and is most useful when users need a deeper view of soil biological communities.

title: Bulk Density Sampling
text: Bulk density sampling uses volumetric soil cores or equivalent undisturbed sampling approaches to estimate how much dry soil mass is contained in a given volume.

title: Bulk Density Sampling
text:
Main target variables

bulk density,
support for stock calculations,
support for compaction and structure-related interpretation.

title: Bulk Density Sampling
text:
Descriptor groups supported

compaction,
structure,
soil organic carbon stock interpretation,
and deeper reference assessment.

title: Aggregate Stability
text: Aggregate stability methods assess how well soil aggregates resist breakdown when exposed to water or disturbance.

title: Aggregate Stability
text:
Main target variables

aggregate stability,
slaking behaviour,
structural resistance to breakdown.

title: Aggregate Stability
text:
Descriptor groups supported

structure,
water behaviour,
erosion-related interpretation,
and practical comparison between sites or treatments.

title: Digital Services
text: Digital services are the layer that connects measurements, observations, contextual information, and outputs.

title: Digital Services
text:
AI4SoilHealth App
The app provides a user-facing environment for viewing, storing, and interacting with soil-health information.

Key roles of the app include:

combining contextual layers with local information,
supporting field data entry,
storing observations and uploaded information,
and connecting users with visual outputs.

title: Digital Services
text:
Field data collection modules
These modules support geolocated recording of:

field observations,
sample locations,
photos,
and selected measured variables.

title: Digital Services
text:
Dashboard and visualisation interface
The dashboard helps users inspect maps, tables, and site-level information.

title: Digital Services
text:
Reporting outputs
The digital toolbox can support the creation of user-facing summaries, including soil-health cards and related reporting outputs.

title: Digital Services
text:
Supporting data layer
The toolbox is supported by a broader digital data and visualisation environment. This background layer helps users place local observations into a wider context by providing maps, layers, and comparative information.

For communication purposes, it is useful to present this supporting data environment as a horizontal or enabling service that helps power the toolbox, rather than as the toolbox itself.

title: Contact and Contributions
text: This starter site is designed for collaborative refinement.

title: Contact and Contributions
text:
What partners can help improve
Partners can contribute by:

reviewing the wording of individual tool pages,
adding or replacing photos,
refining the list of target variables,
clarifying who the most likely end-users are,
and improving the practical notes and limitations for each method.

title: Contact and Contributions
text:
Priority content requests
The most helpful next contributions are:

a preferred image for each tool,
confirmation of the key variables measured by each tool,
feedback on how mature each tool is for end-user communication,
and comments on whether any important toolbox component is still missing.

title: What is the AI4SoilHealth Toolbox?
text: The AI4SoilHealth Toolbox is a practical end-user environment for soil health assessment and monitoring. It combines measurement methods, supporting equipment, digital workflows, and reporting functions so that users can move from a soil-health question to a usable result.

title: What is the AI4SoilHealth Toolbox?
text:
Scope
The toolbox supports soil health assessment across several themes, including:

salinity and soil chemical condition,
soil organic carbon and broader soil fertility,
soil structure and compaction,
infiltration and water behaviour,
nutrient-related variables,
biological activity and biodiversity,
and field-to-digital recording and interpretation.

title: What is the AI4SoilHealth Toolbox?
text:
What the toolbox is not
The toolbox should not be understood as a single instrument or one standalone app. It is a collection of complementary components that work together.

title: What is the AI4SoilHealth Toolbox?
text:
Main layers of the toolbox

1. Field and rapid-assessment tools
These include methods and instruments that can be used directly in the field or close to the field, such as spectroscopy, infiltration tests, visual structure assessments, salinity and pH screening, and biological observation methods.


2. Laboratory methods
These include more detailed analyses such as standard laboratory soil analysis, eDNA/metabarcoding, enzymatic activity measurements, and reference measurements used to support interpretation and validation.


3. Digital services
These include the AI4SoilHealth app, geolocated data-entry modules, visualisation interfaces, and reporting outputs such as soil-health summaries and cards.

title: What is the AI4SoilHealth Toolbox?
text:
Why this matters to end-users
Different users do not need the same level of complexity. A farmer may need a fast view of a few priority variables and a simple report. A researcher or advisor may need a combination of field tools, laboratory methods, and contextual mapping. The toolbox is intended to support both kinds of use by linking methods to clear outputs.

title: What is the AI4SoilHealth Toolbox?
text:
Typical user questions
Users may approach the toolbox with questions such as:

Is this soil showing signs of salinity or nutrient imbalance?
How can I assess soil structure and infiltration in the field?
Which tools can help me look at biodiversity or biological functioning?
How can I combine local observations with digital layers and visual outputs?
What is the most appropriate tool for a rapid assessment, and what requires laboratory support?

title: Soil Health Descriptors
text: This page helps users start from the soil-health issue of interest rather than from the tool itself.

title: Soil Health Descriptors
section: Descriptor groups supported by the toolbox
text:
Descriptor groups supported by the toolbox

Salinity and chemical imbalance
Relevant tools include salinity / pH / EC methods, multi-parameter field sensors, standard laboratory analysis, and supporting digital comparison tools.


Soil organic carbon and soil condition
Relevant tools include spectroscopy, standard laboratory analysis, bulk density sampling, and digital comparison/reporting tools.


Soil structure and compaction
Relevant tools include VESS, aggregate stability methods, bulk density sampling, infiltration testing, and selected field sensors.


Nutrient-related variables
Relevant tools include standard laboratory analysis, field pH/EC/salinity methods, and multi-parameter field probes.


Water behaviour and reduced water retention
Relevant tools include infiltration testing, structure-related tools, moisture-related sensors, and contextual digital layers.


Biodiversity and biological functioning
Relevant tools include eDNA/metabarcoding, macrofauna and earthworm observation, MicroBIOMETER, and enzymatic activity methods.

title: Soil Health Descriptors
text:
Quick mapping: descriptor to tools







Descriptor / issue
Useful toolbox components




Salinity
Salinity / pH / EC methods, field probes, lab analysis, app and dashboard


Soil organic carbon
Spectroscopy, lab analysis, bulk density sampling, digital comparison


Structure
VESS, aggregate stability, infiltration testing, visual records


Compaction
Bulk density sampling, VESS, selected in-field probes


Nutrient status
Lab analysis, pH/EC methods, field probes


Water retention / behaviour
Infiltration testing, structure methods, moisture-related tools


Biodiversity
eDNA, macrofauna observation, MicroBIOMETER, enzymatic activity


Biological functioning
Enzymatic activity, eDNA, macrofauna, MicroBIOMETER

title: Soil Health Descriptors
text:
Practical note
Not every descriptor is assessed with the same level of maturity or simplicity. Some methods are easier to deploy broadly, while others are more specialised and may require laboratory support or expert interpretation.

title: AI4SoilHealth Toolbox
text: The AI4SoilHealth Toolbox is a user-oriented collection of field methods, laboratory approaches, digital tools, and supporting data services that help users observe, assess, interpret, and communicate soil health.

title: AI4SoilHealth Toolbox
section: What users can do with the toolbox
text:
What users can do with the toolbox


Assess multiple soil health descriptors
Use targeted methods for salinity, soil organic carbon, structure, nutrient status, water behaviour, and biodiversity.


Combine field, lab, and digital information
Move from direct measurements and observations to maps, records, and reports.


Choose methods that fit the use case
Some tools support rapid screening, while others provide more detailed or reference-style assessment.


Generate usable outputs
View maps, track observations, compare sites, and produce soil-health summaries.

title: AI4SoilHealth Toolbox
text:
Who the toolbox is for
The toolbox is designed for a broad range of users, including farmers, land managers, advisors, researchers, environmental professionals, and public authorities. Different users may rely on different parts of the toolbox. Some will focus on rapid in-field methods, while others will combine field sampling with laboratory analysis and digital interpretation.

title: AI4SoilHealth Toolbox
AI4SoilHealth Toolbox
section: What is included in the toolbox
text:
What is included in the toolbox
The toolbox brings together three complementary layers:

Field tools and methods for direct observations and rapid measurements.
Laboratory approaches for deeper analysis and validation.
Digital tools and services for data entry, visualisation, comparison, and reporting.


The toolbox is supported by a broader digital data environment. This supporting layer provides contextual maps, background information, and visualisation services, but it is not the same thing as the toolbox itself.

title: AI4SoilHealth Toolbox
text:
Suggested first build priorities
For a first public release, the most important content is:

a clear toolbox definition,
a well-structured catalogue of tools,
a descriptor-to-tool mapping,
a simple user workflow,
and a clear explanation of the digital services around the toolbox.

title: AI4SoilHealth App
text: The AI4SoilHealth App is the main user-facing digital environment around the toolbox. It helps users bring together field observations, uploaded information, contextual layers, and visual outputs.

title: AI4SoilHealth App
section: What users can do with it
text:
What users can do with it

view available layers and contextual information,
store or explore local observations,
connect measurements to locations,
inspect maps and tables,
and support communication through digital outputs.

title: AI4SoilHealth App
text:
Relevant descriptor groups
The app can support all main descriptor groups by acting as the place where information is organised, explored, and presented.

title: Supporting Data Layer
text: The toolbox is supported by a broader digital data and visualisation layer that provides contextual information, mapped layers, and comparison services.

title: Supporting Data Layer
text:
Why it matters
Users often need more than one measurement. They also need to know how a site sits within a broader spatial context. The supporting data layer helps with that task.

title: Supporting Data Layer
text:
Communication note
For end-user communication, it is usually best to present this as a supporting or horizontal service that powers part of the toolbox, rather than as the toolbox itself.

title: Tool Catalogue
text:
This catalogue presents the main toolbox components in end-user language. Each entry explains what the tool is, what it is mainly used for, and which soil-health variables or descriptor groups it can support.



AI4SoilHealth App


Digital environment for viewing, storing, and interacting with soil-health information.




Soil Spectroscopy


Rapid, non-destructive estimation of several soil properties.




Standard Soil Laboratory Analysis


Reference measurements for key physicochemical variables.




Bulk Density Sampling


Sampling approach to support structure and compaction-related assessment.




VESS


Visual evaluation of soil structure in the field.




Aggregate Stability


Simple methods for assessing structural stability and slaking behaviour.




Infiltration Test


Field method to assess water entry and related hydraulic behaviour.




Salinity / pH / EC Methods


Rapid assessment of chemical condition and salinity-related issues.




SEAR / Digit Soil


Rapid assessment of extracellular enzymatic activity.




MicroBIOMETER


Kit-based view of microbial biomass and the fungal/bacterial balance.




eDNA / Metabarcoding


Laboratory workflow for biodiversity and biological-community analysis.




Macrofauna Observation


Observation and image-based support for earthworms and soil fauna.




Supporting Data Layer


Horizontal data and visualisation service that supports the toolbox.

title: Standard Soil Laboratory Analysis
text: Standard laboratory soil analysis provides the reference layer for many toolbox assessments. It is the most familiar route for measuring key chemical and physical variables in a structured way.

title: Standard Soil Laboratory Analysis
text:
Main target variables

pH,
electrical conductivity,
phosphorus,
nitrogen,
texture fractions,
cation exchange capacity,
soil organic carbon,
and related variables depending on the analysis package.

title: Standard Soil Laboratory Analysis
text:
Descriptor groups supported

salinity,
nutrient-related variables,
soil organic carbon,
acidity,
and part of the baseline descriptor set used for broader assessment.

title: MicroBIOMETER
text: MicroBIOMETER is a kit-based approach that provides a practical view of microbial biomass and the fungal/bacterial balance in soil.

title: MicroBIOMETER
text:
Main target variables

microbial biomass carbon,
fungi fraction,
bacteria fraction.

title: MicroBIOMETER
text:
Descriptor groups supported

biodiversity,
biological functioning,
and support for soil biological interpretation.

title: SEAR / Digit Soil
text: SEAR, developed in the Digit Soil pathway, is a rapid way to assess extracellular enzymatic activity in soil.

title: SEAR / Digit Soil
text:
Main target variables
SEAR focuses on several targeted enzyme groups, including:

β-glucosaminidase,
β-glucosidase,
phosphatases,
β-xylosidase,
and leucine aminopeptidase.

title: SEAR / Digit Soil
text:
Descriptor groups supported

biological functioning,
nutrient cycling support,
and broader soil biological activity.

title: VESS – Visual Evaluation of Soil Structure
text: VESS is a field-based method for visually assessing soil structure quality.

title: VESS – Visual Evaluation of Soil Structure
text:
Main target variables

structural quality,
visible signs of compaction,
root and aggregate condition,
and practical field interpretation.

title: VESS – Visual Evaluation of Soil Structure
text:
Descriptor groups supported

structure,
compaction,
and support for water-behaviour interpretation.
